Ryan Grant.

Most marketing grads have internships. I have a company I built from nothing, scaled to 300+ clients, and sold. Before I finished my degree. I've spent two years earning shelf space from skeptical store managers, written $12.5MM media strategies, and I'm now finishing my MSc at Clemson while helping businesses deploy AI that actually saves them time. I don't need a runway. I need a problem worth solving and the latitude to own it.

The Honest Version

Why I'm
different.

Most people my age are still figuring out what they want to do. I've already built a business, scaled it, and sold it. Started O'Nattys Apparel at 19 with no funding, no team, and no roadmap. Just a product I believed in and a willingness to figure the rest out as I went. Two years later I had 300+ wholesale clients across Canada, two full collections, and a buyer at the table. I took the deal when I was ready for something bigger. That decision taught me more about business than anything else on this page.

Since then I've gone deeper, academically and practically. A 3.7 GPA at Guelph's Lang School, two summers in the field at Muskoka Brewery managing 100+ retail accounts and running brand activations from street festivals to Costco, a $12.5MM Amazon DSP strategy built from scratch, behavioral economics research on how people actually make decisions, and now an MSc at Clemson focused on analytics, consumer behavior, and AI strategy. I don't just study how businesses grow. I've done it, and I know what it takes.

Right now I'm focused on the intersection of marketing strategy and AI automation. Specifically, helping businesses identify where they're losing time and building systems that give it back. I thrive when there's pressure, real ownership, and something worth executing on. I'm based in Clemson, available from May 2026, and genuinely open to the right opportunity anywhere. I also built the Clemson MSc Marketing Hub for our cohort. Because I build things, that's just what I do.

Builder & Operator

Not a consultant who recommends and leaves. I execute. From cold-calling wholesale buyers at 19 to building Costco placements. I care about what actually moves the number.

Data by Default

Power BI, SPSS, R Studio, ESRI, Amazon DSP. I use data to build a real argument — not to produce charts that sit in decks nobody reads.

AI in Production

My current focus. I find where teams are losing time and build systems that fix it. GenAI, prompt engineering, HubSpot AI, Zapier, Make. Practical, not theoretical.

Founder's Lens

I think like a founder. That means cross-functional ownership, comfort with ambiguity, and a bias toward building — not waiting to be told what to do.

Case Studies · Strategy & Research

How I
solve problems.

Amazon DSP · Paid Media
Awakening the Flywheel. Day 1 Strategy for Gratitude Mattresses
Revenue looked great on the surface. Underneath, the customer base was eroding.
$12.5M
Budget Proposed
+20%
Organic Target
Revenue up 79.8% sounds like a win, until you realize it was entirely driven by a 38.7% price hike. Unit volume was down 14.7%. Page views down 17.2%. The customer base was quietly shrinking while the P&L looked healthy. With 91% of budget locked in conversion and zero spend on Audio, Twitch, or Fire TV, channels running 90%+ new-to-brand rates, the brand was harvesting its existing audience and calling it growth. I built a case for a 25% budget increase to $12.5MM, restructured across a real funnel: $5MM Awareness to reach 90M+ furniture shoppers who'd never considered a mattress, $3.8MM Consideration, and $1.2MM Conversion plus retargeting across the 2–4 week window most buyers actually need. The argument: you can't cut your way to organic growth.
Behavioral Economics · Experimental Design
TD Bank. Default Nudge Strategy to Drive Savings Participation
People don't do what's best for them. But they reliably do what's easiest. Design for the path of least resistance.
600
Simulated Participants
P<.005
Simulated Significance
2-Yr
Quasi-Experiment
40% of Canadian millennials have less than $500 saved. TD's deposit balances were declining as consumers chose spending over saving — reducing TD's lending capacity and revenue spread. The intervention: change the default. Instead of requiring customers to opt in to payroll savings transfers (low participation due to procrastination and decision fatigue), new TD student chequing accounts automatically transfer 5% of each payroll deposit to a high-interest savings account, with a frictionless opt-out. Study design: a 2-year quasi-experiment with 600 participants split into Condition A (control, observed only) and Condition B (5% auto-transfer). Year 1 establishes baseline savings behavior across covariates (income, expenses, household size, financial goals). Year 2 introduces the intervention and monitors quarterly. ANCOVA analysis controls for individual covariates. Two supporting studies anchor the framework — Madrian & Shea's 401(k) auto-enrollment research (default participation dramatically outperforms opt-in) and a Netherlands supermarket food donation field study demonstrating that default nudges increase target behavior without diminishing experienced autonomy. Simulated H1 result: statistically significant increase in savings rates between control and treatment groups (P<.005), with covariate influences non-significant (p=0.42, p=0.90, p=0.63). Simulated H2 result: treatment group reported significantly higher financial decision satisfaction post-experiment (P<.005) vs. no change in control (P=0.189). Conclusion: smart defaults beat cash bonuses and awareness campaigns.
Marketing Strategy · Design-Build · Programmatic
Atelier Design & Development. A $250K to $500K Revenue Strategy
A design-build firm in one of the fastest-growing markets in the US, stalling at $250K. The answer wasn’t more clients — it was a sharper position and a plan to reach the right ones.
2×
Revenue Target
60
Project Capacity/Yr
Atelier Design & Development is a Greenville, SC design-build firm operating in a market that is actively booming — South Carolina ranks #1 for inbound migration in 2025, Greenville new listings are up 21.9%, and population is growing 1.4% year-over-year. The firm was generating ~$250K/year with no formal marketing and no access to the developer segment that could double its revenue. As part of our MSc consulting team, I contributed to the full go-to-market build. Customer intelligence segmented two distinct targets: homeowner clients ("Sally from the Block" — 34–45, $100K+ income, 4-hour radius, referral-motivated) and the high-value developer segment ("Dan the Developer" — large-scale builders in FL and TN, motivated by proven KPIs and portfolio volume). Market sizing showed a SAM of $400M–$1.2B within a 1-hour radius (13,000–26,000 major renovation projects/year at $20K–$60K average). Revenue roadmap to $500K: 18 mid-size projects at ~$19,100 each, 740 billable hours at $180/hr, and rush-fee premiums. Programmatic geofencing strategy targets developers browsing renovation content in high-growth ZIP codes. Social strategy: LinkedIn growth from 904 to 2,000 followers in 6 months. Positioning: design-forward, European-inspired, transparent pricing — the integrated design-build alternative that eliminates the fragmented design-bid-build workflow that puts 70–80% of renovations over budget.
GTM Strategy · B2B Manufacturing
Vansco Inc. GTM Strategy for a 95%-Dependent Manufacturer
95% of revenue from one client. No marketing budget. No digital presence. We built a complete growth strategy from scratch.
95%
Client Concentration
<75%
2028 Target
$650M
SAM Identified
Vansco is a 28-year-old wire harness manufacturer in Greenwood, SC with a structural problem: 95% of revenue tied to a single client (Eaton), zero marketing presence, and no SEO visibility. As market research lead on a 4-person MSc consulting team, I built Vansco's first formal GTM strategy. We mapped the full market from TAM ($1.7T global semiconductor/electronic parts market; $15.6B US segment) through a SAM of $350M–$650M within a 3-hour radius, and sized the SOM at $400K–$900K/year from 15–25 new contracts annually. Competitive positioning placed Vansco in the white space between high-volume commodity manufacturers and slow defense-specialized shops — a high-mix, low-to-mid volume niche larger players cannot serve efficiently. Deliverables: positioning statement, brand guidelines, Google Business Profile buildout, SEO and backlink strategy, LinkedIn and Facebook content calendar, and a trade show roadmap targeting robotics integrators, solar manufacturing, and AI data infrastructure verticals. Objective: top-3 search visibility in Upstate SC by Q4 2026 and Eaton dependency below 75% by 2028.

Est. 2021 · Toronto, ON

O'Nattys
Apparel —
Founded,
Scaled & Sold.

I was 19, had no funding and no business background, and decided to start a clothing brand anyway. That's the kind of decision that either teaches you everything or teaches you why people don't do it. For me, it was everything.

Two years of running every part of the business myself, product design in Photoshop, Meta ad campaigns on a shoestring, building a wholesale pipeline from cold calls, supplier negotiations, Mailchimp sequences, B2B outreach. Entrepreneurship gave me a cross-functional view of business that no degree could replicate. By the time I was ready to move on, O'Nattys had 300+ wholesale accounts across Canada and two full collections. I found a buyer, negotiated the deal, and completed a strategic exit in 2023. I don't regret a single part of it, including the decision to leave.
